questions.text: You notice a colleague submitting a falsified expense report. What do you do?

options.text: Ignore it — it is not your concern
Tell other colleagues about it
Report it to a supervisor through the appropriate channel
Confront your colleague aggressively in public

questions.text: Your manager asks you to record a transaction inaccurately to meet monthly targets. You should:

options.text: Do it — your manager told you to
Refuse and explain your concern professionally, escalate if pressed
Do it this once and report it later
Resign immediately

questions.text: You accidentally sent a confidential client document to the wrong email. You should:

options.text: Delete the sent email and hope no one notices
Immediately notify your supervisor and the client, and follow the data breach protocol
Wait to see if the recipient contacts you
Ask a colleague to handle it

questions.text: A colleague takes credit for your work in a team meeting. The best response is to:

options.text: Publicly embarrass them in the meeting
Say nothing and let it pass
Calmly and professionally clarify your contribution at the appropriate moment
Complain to everyone except the person involved

questions.text: You are offered a gift by a vendor whose contract you are reviewing. You should:

options.text: Accept it — it's just a gesture of goodwill
Accept it but disclose it to your manager
Decline and report the offer according to your organization's conflict-of-interest policy
Accept and keep it private

questions.text: You realize you made a mistake that affected a key deliverable. What do you do?

options.text: Hide it and hope it resolves itself
Blame external factors
Acknowledge the mistake, notify the relevant people, and propose a corrective action
Wait for someone else to notice
